items.q: How do you say 'my house' in French?
items.why: Students use mon as default. French possessives agree with the OBJECT's gender, not the owner. Maison is feminine, so MA maison. (Note: mon goes before feminine nouns starting with a vowel - mon amie.)

items.correct: As a nasal vowel - the n is not fully pronounced.
items.why: Students pronounce nasal vowels like English consonants. French 'on', 'an', 'in', 'un' are nasalised vowels - air resonates through the nose but the n consonant itself isn't articulated.

items.q: When does the past participle agree with avoir verbs?
items.correct: When the direct object comes BEFORE the verb (la pomme que j'ai mangée).
items.why: Students learn 'avoir = no agreement' as a blanket rule. The exception is PRECEDING DIRECT OBJECT: 'Je l'ai mangée' (la pomme) requires the participle to agree with the object pronoun.
It's Digital divide. “Broken phone” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which tense suits hypotheticals after 'si' + imparfait?
items.why: Correct answer: Conditional. “Future” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate percentage of French households with internet access?
items.why: The real figure is 92. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
